turns.content: I'm trying to build a generic utility that works with closures of any lifetime, but I keep running into errors where the compiler says it can't prove the closure lives long enough. What is the specific mechanism in Rust that allows me to write code that works for any lifetime without being tied to a single variable?

You are dealing with Higher-Ranked Trait Bounds, which is the Rust equivalent of quantifying over lifetimes like forall<'a>. This allows you to define traits that accept closures and guarantee they work regardless of the specific lifetime bound. The syntax for this in Rust uses a special loop operator called for<'a> inside trait definitions.

Okay, so I can use for<'a> to say my function works with any closure. But why does the compiler still complain about lifetimes even after I add that syntax? Is there something wrong with how I'm structuring my generic function?

The issue is likely that your function isn't declaring the HRTB constraint properly on the trait bound or in the generic parameters. When using a generic type parameter, you must explicitly add for<'a> to the lifetime constraints so the compiler knows it should treat the closure as having any possible lifetime.
I added for<'a> but I still get an error saying my function is not generic enough. Can you show me exactly how to modify a trait definition so that a closure like Box<dyn Fn()> actually compiles?

You need to declare the HRTB on the trait itself using for<'a> in the where clause. For example, you can define a trait called MyTrait with an impl block that takes a closure and uses for<'a> to ensure the closure is treated as generic over lifetimes.
That looks better but I'm still getting errors when I try to pass a closure. Is it possible that the compiler doesn't support HRTBs in all trait implementations?

HRTBs are fully supported, but they require careful usage of generics and where clauses. You must ensure your trait implementation explicitly includes the for<'a> syntax in its bounds so that any closure passed will be accepted regardless of its lifetime.
I've tried adding for<'a> to my Fn trait bound but I'm still getting errors. Is there another way to make a function generic over lifetimes without using HRTBs?

There is no alternative to HRTBs if you need true generality over arbitrary lifetimes for closures. The only correct approach is to use the for<'a> syntax in your trait definition or function bounds, otherwise the compiler will reject any closure that isn't explicitly tied to a specific lifetime.
